text:
cli.py has grown to ~590 lines - by far the largest engine module (next is
migrate at ~310). It accreted across many workstreams: flag parsing, the verb
catalog, human + JSON renderers, error/result emitters, backing selection, and
one long main() that dispatches every verb inline via a chain of `if cmd ==
...` blocks. It still works and is well-tested, but it is no longer easy for a
HUMAN to read, navigate, or extend - a new verb means threading through a long
function, and related logic (a verb's parsing + action + output) is scattered.

This workstream restructures cli.py for human maintainability WITHOUT changing
behaviour. The CLI's external contract - verbs, flags, stdout/stderr (human and
--json), exit codes - stays identical; only the internal organisation improves.
Likely shapes (a discovery/planning decision): split into focused modules
(e.g. dispatch, rendering, errors, catalog) and/or a per-verb handler structure
so each verb's concerns live together, with main() reduced to thin routing.

Non-negotiables carried in: behaviour-preserving (the full suite stays green,
no test assertions changed except mechanical import moves); dependency-free
(stdlib only); deterministic; reuse before adding; ceremony must buy back its
cost - a refactor that doesn't clearly improve readability isn't worth it.

Done-when: cli.py's responsibilities are separated into coherent, navigable
units (smaller modules and/or a per-verb handler registry) so adding or
changing a verb is local rather than threading a long function; main() is thin
routing; the external CLI contract and all outputs are byte-for-byte unchanged;
the full test suite passes with only mechanical edits. The concrete target
structure is a discovery/planning decision; discovery may find part of the file
is already fine and scope the split to what actually helps.
title: CLI Refactoring
